status: DEV — clinician_review pending; NOT patient-facing; reference-only (engine-isolated). Whether a solid oral form may be crushed/split/dispersed (APF22 Modification of oral formulation). Carries NO dose. Records author to review_status 'draft'; require clinician verification against source + AMH/TGA.

attestation.statement: administration_handling reference register — APF22-cited facts / public primary sources; NOT prescribing, NOT a dose source.

records.rationale: crushing destroys the modified-release matrix, causing dose dumping and overdose risk
records.alternative: use an immediate-release liquid under prescriber direction

records.rationale: bisphosphonate with a local mucosal irritant effect; crushing raises the risk of oesophageal irritation/ulceration
records.alternative: consider an IV bisphosphonate (e.g. zoledronic acid) or an oral-suspension bisphosphonate where a solid form cannot be swallowed

records.rationale: enteric coating limits local gastric irritation; crushing negates it
records.alternative: diclofenac suppository or topical gel, or an oral-suspension NSAID (e.g. naproxen), are available

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

records.rationale: cytotoxic/hazardous drug — crushing generates powder that poses an occupational exposure and contamination hazard
records.alternative: handle only with appropriate precautions; seek a suitable liquid or alternative agent for patients who cannot swallow tablets

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'
provenance.authored_by: claude-chat clinical review (APF22-cited facts; wording original)

records.rationale: cytotoxic/hazardous drug — crushing creates hazardous powder (occupational exposure risk) and impairs accurate dose delivery
records.alternative: parenteral methotrexate is available where an oral solid cannot be given; handle with cytotoxic precautions

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

records.alternative: an oral liquid is available; immediate-release tablets may be crushed but formulations are not bioequivalent

records.alternative: consider an antidepressant available as an immediate-release or dispersible form (e.g. fluoxetine); escitalopram oral solution is available

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

records.rationale: enteric coating targets release to the lower bowel; crushing causes gastric irritation and loss of intended action
records.alternative: bisacodyl suppositories, or alternative laxatives in liquid/enema form, are available

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

records.alternative: an orally dispersible tablet is available

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'

provenance.source: APF22 (facts, cited) — Section A Table A.4 'Examples of medicines that should not be crushed'
